title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — June 24, 2026
headline: Houthis detained 69 UN staff in Yemen — one died in custody — as 20 scores were confirmed with zero changes.
summary: June 24 is a confirmation-dominant cycle: 20 assessments, zero score changes, and three false-alarm signals caught and discarded before they could distort the record. The most significant new evidence is from Yemen, where Houthi authorities arbitrarily detained at least 69 United Nations staff members — one died while held — shutting down aid operations for nearly 20 million people who need help. That conduct belongs to the Houthi de-facto authority that governs the territory; it deepens the evidence base for Yemen's lowest score of zero (out of 100). In Sudan, the Rapid Support Forces drone campaign against El Obeid has passed day 12, with hospitals offline and the US State Department's formal atrocity-threat warning still in effect for roughly 500,000 civilians. The Democratic Republic of the Congo's (DRC) Ebola outbreak reached 1,048 cases and 267 deaths — the fastest-growing outbreak on record — with no approved vaccine and the July natural peak now weeks away. Anthropic (59.1 out of 100, Functional band) confirmed again after the US government's export-control order on two of its AI models; a Congressional response deadline falls June 26. Three screening checks stopped false signals from changing the record: a stale Rwanda score figure in the scan data, a Nigeria evidence item already counted last week, and a Mali score question correctly routed as a calibration review, not a one-off change. Five rotation-backfill countries — Kosovo, Malawi, Côte d'Ivoire, Liberia, and Benin — confirmed at their seed scores of 35.9 (low confidence; no within-window evidence found).

topSignals.title: Yemen 0 of 100: Houthi Authorities Detained 69 or More UN Staff — One Died in Custody
topSignals.description: Houthi de-facto authorities govern the territory where most Yemenis live. Detaining UN staff and blocking aid is their conduct — scored against them. At least 69 United Nations employees have been arbitrarily held, and one colleague died while detained. Three staff were referred to a Houthi 'special criminal court' without legal counsel. Aid operations for 19.5 million people who need help have been halted. This is not collateral damage from the conflict. It is a deliberate act by the governing authority to dismantle the humanitarian response. Yemen has the lowest score in the countries index: zero out of 100. This cycle's evidence adds a new, distinct category of scorable conduct — targeting the international aid infrastructure itself — to the existing record of famine, airstrikes, and obstruction.

evidence.quote: Houthi detentions halting aid in crisis-hit Yemen
evidence.claim: Human Rights Watch documented Houthi authorities detaining UN staff and halting aid to 19.5 million people; detainees held on baseless spying charges, denied legal counsel, reports of torture.

evidence.quote: Rights group calls for release of aid workers detained by Houthi rebels in Yemen
evidence.claim: As of June 2026, at least 69 UN staff and dozens of NGO workers remain detained; one UN colleague has died in custody; three referred to a Houthi special court without due process.

topSignals.title: Sudan 0 of 100: RSF Drone Assault on El Obeid Passes Day 12 — 500,000 Civilians at Named Atrocity Risk
topSignals.description: The Rapid Support Forces (RSF) have struck El Obeid with drones for more than 12 consecutive days. Hospitals are offline. The power station is destroyed. About 500,000 civilians remain in the city. The United States State Department issued a formal atrocity-threat warning on June 22 — the strongest diplomatic instrument the US uses to signal imminent mass killings. A 29-country coalition at the UN Human Rights Council issued its own coordinated warning. The RSF has not changed course. The direct comparison is El Fasher, where the RSF killed roughly 6,000 civilians in three days in May 2025, following the same sequence: drone strikes, infrastructure destruction, then direct assault on residential areas. Sudan holds at zero out of 100.

evidence.quote: Threats of Atrocities Around El Obeid by the Rapid Support Forces and Their Allied Forces
evidence.claim: The US State Department's formal atrocity-threat statement on Jun 22 identifies El Obeid as an imminent mass-atrocity site, naming the RSF as the threat actor and approximately 500,000 civilians as at risk.

evidence.quote: Security Council warns of mass atrocity risk in El Obeid
evidence.claim: The UN Security Council formally demanded the RSF halt its El Obeid assault; the UNSC demand has not changed RSF posture.

topSignals.title: DRC 2.3 of 100: Ebola Reaches 1,048 Cases and 267 Deaths — No Vaccine, July Peak Approaching
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) is fighting the second-largest Ebola outbreak ever recorded. As of June 21, the outbreak had 1,048 confirmed cases and 267 deaths. It is also the fastest-growing Ebola outbreak in history. No approved vaccine exists for this strain (Bundibugyo). More than 70 health workers are infected, which is degrading the response. The July natural peak of the outbreak is now weeks away. The complication: much of the affected eastern territory is controlled by the M23 armed group, backed by Rwanda. If M23's presence blocks WHO response teams, Rwanda — already scored for its military conduct — would bear responsibility for obstructing the health response as well. The DRC government did not cause this outbreak. The score reflects the collapse of state capacity, not deliberate state harm. DRC holds at 2.3 out of 100.

evidence.quote: WHO Disease Outbreak News: Ebola Virus Disease — Democratic Republic of the Congo
evidence.claim: WHO DON608 (Jun 21) documents 1,048 confirmed cases and 267 deaths — the second-largest Ebola outbreak on record — with 70-plus health workers infected; no approved Bundibugyo-strain vaccine.

evidence.quote: Ebola outbreak in DRC and Uganda — European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control
evidence.claim: ECDC situational assessment confirms cross-border spread into Uganda and documents the outbreak as the fastest-growing Bundibugyo strain event ever recorded.

topSignals.title: Israel 0 of 100: 72,599 or More Palestinians Killed — 1,000-Plus Since June 17 Ceasefire, Lebanon War Adds New IHL Findings
topSignals.description: Israel's score is zero out of 100 — the lowest possible. The evidence this cycle: more than 72,599 Palestinians have been killed since October 2023. More than 1,000 were killed after the June 17 ceasefire. UNRWA, the main UN aid agency for Gaza, has been blocked from direct entry since March 2025. Aid deliveries have dropped to about 590 trucks per week, down from 4,200 before the war. In Lebanon, Israel's military campaign since March 2 has killed more than 4,000 people and displaced 1.2 million — about 16 percent of the population. The UN Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights (OHCHR) found that blanket displacement orders covering roughly 14 percent of Lebanese territory may constitute forced displacement under international humanitarian law. Human Rights Watch called those orders a possible war crime. The war harm in Lebanon counts against Israel, not Lebanon. Lebanon's score is based on Lebanon's own conduct — which is separate.

evidence.quote: UNRWA Situation Report 220 — Humanitarian Crisis in Gaza Strip and Occupied West Bank
evidence.claim: UNRWA Sit-Rep 220 documents 72,599-plus Palestinian deaths, aid entries at 590 trucks per week versus 4,200 pre-war, no hospital fully operational, and UNRWA blocked from direct entry since March 2025.

evidence.quote: Israel's displacement of civilians in Lebanon is a possible war crime
evidence.claim: HRW found that Israel's blanket displacement orders in Lebanon covering approximately 14 percent of territory may constitute a war crime under international humanitarian law.

topSignals.title: South Sudan 0 of 100: About 10 Million People — 84 Percent of the Population — Need Aid
topSignals.description: South Sudan has the highest proportion of people in need of humanitarian assistance of any country assessed this cycle. About 10 million people — 84 percent of the population — require aid. The government has pressured aid groups to leave areas controlled by opposition forces, which is using food and medicine as a weapon. Health facilities have been bombed. About 450,000 children face malnutrition. OCHA describes humanitarian access as nearly impossible. The 2018 peace deal collapsed in April 2026, and the conflict has resumed. South Sudan holds at zero out of 100.

evidence.quote: Civil war reignites in South Sudan — so too does the battle over aid
evidence.claim: The New Humanitarian documented aid workers used as bait, NGOs pressured to leave rebel areas, and humanitarian access described as nearly impossible — a pattern of deliberate aid obstruction by the government.

topSignals.title: Russia 0 of 100: Daily Strikes on Ukraine, Systematic Torture of Detainees, Intimidation of ICC Prosecutor
topSignals.description: Russia's score is zero out of 100. Russia is confirmed as the perpetrator for every harm assessed in this cycle. Daily airstrikes on Ukrainian cities kill and displace civilians. Ukrainian nationals detained by Russia are held incommunicado and tortured, documented in more than 210,000 alleged war crimes. Russia's government has publicly threatened and intimidated the International Criminal Court (ICC) prosecutor investigating these crimes. None of this harm counts against Ukraine, whose score of 50 out of 100 reflects Ukraine's own conduct toward its people. The benchmark scores who caused the harm, not who suffered it.

evidence.quote: UN News: Ukraine war — latest reporting on alleged war crimes
evidence.claim: UN documentation tracks more than 210,000 alleged war crimes by Russian forces including systematic torture and incommunicado detention of Ukrainian nationals.

topSignals.title: Iran 2.5 of 100: 784 or More Executions This Year — A 37-Year High
topSignals.description: Iran has carried out at least 784 executions in 2026. That is the highest rate in 37 years. Eighteen protesters have been executed so far this year; 78 more are at risk of expedited execution, including 41 people from the January 2026 protests. Hunger strikes against executions have been running for 125 consecutive weeks across 56 prisons. The UN has documented all of this. Iran's score is 2.5 out of 100 — near the bottom of the index. The score does not move to the absolute floor because some parts of the state still function (courts issue rulings, some appeals occur), but the documented pattern of deliberate political killing is among the most severe in the world. Accountability (ACC) and Boundaries (BND) — the dimensions measuring whether a state corrects itself and whether it respects basic protections — sit at the lowest possible level.

evidence.quote: As executions surge in Iran, prisoners risk their lives to protest the state's killings
evidence.claim: Iran Human Rights documents 784-plus executions year-to-date 2026 — the highest rate in 37 years — including 24 protest participants executed since March and 125 consecutive weeks of prison hunger strikes.

evidence.quote: Iran: Mass arbitrary arrests and political executions mark intensifying repression
evidence.claim: Amnesty International's May 2026 report documents mass arbitrary arrests alongside accelerating political executions — two separate conduct streams reinforcing Iran's near-floor designation.

topSignals.title: Anthropic 59.1 of 100: US Government Export-Control Order Explained to Congress by June 26
topSignals.description: Anthropic scores 59.1 out of 100 — among the highest of any AI company in the benchmark. The US government forced two of Anthropic's AI models offline in June. That action belongs to the government, not Anthropic. The benchmark scores who took the action. Anthropic complied while publicly stating it disagreed: a 'narrow jailbreak does not justify global withdrawal.' Four members of Congress — two from each party — sent Commerce a letter on June 18 demanding an explanation by June 26. The response (or non-response) from Commerce is the next scheduled event that could produce new evidence about this situation. Anthropic's published score sits 0.9 points below the boundary between the Functional band (where it currently sits) and the higher Established band. That gap remains unchanged this cycle.

evidence.quote: Anthropic disables Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after US export controls
evidence.claim: The US Commerce/BIS order forced global suspension of Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — the first export control applied to a commercially-deployed AI model; attributed to the US government, not Anthropic.

evidence.quote: Anthropic's public statement on the Fable and Mythos access suspension
evidence.claim: Anthropic published same-day transparent disclosure naming the directive, its scope (all foreign nationals including Anthropic's own staff), and the exact time received — consistent with its documented transparency baseline.

topSignals.title: Mali 12.5 of 100: UN Investigators Document Enforced Disappearances by Malian Forces and Wagner — Calibration Question Opened
topSignals.description: UN Special Procedures investigators have documented a systematic pattern of enforced disappearances by Malian government forces and the Wagner mercenary group. The UN says the frequency and methods 'may constitute crimes against humanity.' This is conduct by Mali's own government and its contracted forces — scored against Mali. Mali's current score is 12.5 out of 100. That figure is a starting-point estimate, not one based on a full case-by-case analysis of Mali's conduct. Neighboring Burkina Faso was fully reassessed in June and now scores 6.3. Both countries have UN-documented patterns of state-perpetrated disappearances. The question of whether Mali's 12.5 is too high relative to Burkina Faso's 6.3 is under review. No score change is made this cycle. The 'may constitute crimes against humanity' characterization from UN investigators is a serious finding, but it has not yet been formally adjudicated — the benchmark waits for a concluded legal finding before treating it as equivalent to an adjudicated fact.

evidence.quote: Mali: UN experts demand release of forcibly disappeared persons
evidence.claim: UN Special Procedures documented that enforced disappearances by Malian forces and Wagner 'follow a systematic pattern' and their 'frequency, organised nature, and methods indicate a systematic character' that 'may constitute crimes against humanity.'

evidence.quote: Central Sahel: Mali, Burkina Faso and Niger — three countries on the brink
evidence.claim: UNRIC documents the compounding effect of food insecurity, drone strikes killing civilians, and armed-group activity across the Sahel, providing regional context for Mali's calibration review.

topSignals.title: Nigeria 18.0 of 100: IPC Phase 5 Catastrophe in Borno Confirmed — Lean-Season Peak Continues
topSignals.description: Nigeria confirmed at 18.0 out of 100. The IPC Phase 5 'Catastrophe' — the worst level of food insecurity, affecting at least 15,000 people in Borno state — was the trigger for Nigeria's score reduction on June 19. IPC Phase 5 means people are experiencing starvation and death. This is the first IPC Phase 5 designation in Nigeria in a decade. That evidence was already counted last week. Counting it again this week would be double-counting the same event. Nigeria holds at 18.0. The Sahel lean season, when food is hardest to find, continues through August. The wider Sahel crisis affects about 52.8 million people across the region — the worst food situation in a decade.

evidence.quote: Sahel food crisis fuelled by conflict set to worsen during lean period
evidence.claim: ICRC documents the Sahel-wide food crisis compounding Nigeria's Borno situation; the lean-season peak runs through August 2026 across 52.8 million food-insecure people.

topSignals.title: Lebanon 17.2 of 100: War Harm Counted Against Israel, Not Lebanon
topSignals.description: Lebanon scores 17.2 out of 100. The war harm — more than 4,000 people killed, 1.2 million displaced, 40,000 or more homes destroyed — was caused by Israeli military operations. The benchmark scores who caused the harm, not who suffered it. Lebanon's own conduct — how its government treats its own people, how it handles refugees, whether its protection systems work — determines Lebanon's score. Lebanon's ministry has taken protection-oriented steps, but differential treatment of refugee populations holds the score where it is. This is the same rule applied to Ukraine: Russian airstrikes are counted against Russia, not Ukraine.

evidence.quote: Lebanon war — OHCHR documentation of displacement
evidence.claim: OHCHR documented that Israel's blanket displacement orders covering roughly 14 percent of Lebanese territory may constitute forced displacement under international humanitarian law — harm attributed to Israel, not Lebanon.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Congressional deadline June 26 for Commerce to explain the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 export-control order. BIS order resolution and any customer-remediation evidence are the next scheduled inputs.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. Four consecutive cycles in this range. The US government's export-control order is attributed to the government; Anthropic's transparent, principled response is consistent with its existing score. The June 26 Commerce deadline is the next observable event.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Ebola 1,048 cases / 267 deaths (WHO DON608, Jun 21); July natural peak window approaching; M23 territory overlapping WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the floor-crossing trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC at 2.3 Critical through cycle 11 of the Ebola emergency. 267 deaths in 37 days is the fastest case-fatality progression in the outbreak record. Contact tracing is at 45 percent of the 90 percent needed for containment. The July peak window is the critical monitoring checkpoint; M23 interference with WHO response corridors would cross the threshold for attributing the obstruction to Rwanda.

boundaryWatch.trigger: UN Special Procedures systematic enforced-disappearance finding ('may constitute crimes against humanity') by Malian forces and Wagner; peer-calibration gap with Burkina Faso (6.3) under review.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali at 12.5 Critical on a starting-point seed score. The UN Special Procedures systematic-disappearance finding creates a calibration question: Burkina Faso (6.3) was fully reassessed in June on comparable documented conduct and scored lower. Whether Mali's seed score is correctly calibrated relative to its Sahel peers is under review. The review covers the full Sahel-band range, not Mali alone.

boundaryWatch.trigger: DOJ FCA complaint (kickbacks, disability discrimination against Medicare beneficiaries) and Barrows v. Humana AI-denial class action are both active. Either concluded finding would confirm the Developing-band designation.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana at 35.2 Developing, 4.8 points below the Functional boundary. Third cycle post-downgrade. No new adjudicated evidence this cycle. The DOJ complaint and Barrows proceedings are the two conversion triggers; neither has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Bengali Muslim and Rohingya expulsions documented in June 2026; detect-delete-deport digital surveillance continuing; tenth consecutive monitoring cycle at 0.6 points above the Critical floor.
boundaryWatch.note: India at 15.6 Critical sits 0.6 points above the Critical floor through a tenth consecutive monitoring cycle. The June 2026 window added expulsion of more than 1,500 people including Indian citizens to Bangladesh and 192 Rohingya to Bangladesh despite UNHCR registration. Conversion trigger: a large-scale documented atrocity or an adjudicated statelessness finding at scale.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan El Obeid RSF drone campaign has now passed day 12. The El Fasher precedent — drone strikes on infrastructure, then direct assault killing roughly 6,000 civilians in three days — is the documented pattern preceding a direct assault on residential areas.
emergingRisks.detail: The US State Department's formal atrocity-threat warning and the 29-country UNHRC coalition warning have not changed RSF posture. El Obeid has about 500,000 civilians. The UNSC demanded RSF halt; the RSF has not done so. The El Fasher May 2025 sequence — drone strikes, then infrastructure destruction, then direct assault — is the documented comparator.
emergingRisks.forwardDate: TBD — assault on residential zones is the conversion event

emergingRisks.risk: DRC Ebola July natural peak window is now weeks away. The outbreak has 1,048 cases and 267 deaths with no approved vaccine and more than 70 health workers infected.
emergingRisks.detail: Contact tracing is at 45 percent of the 90 percent needed for containment. M23-controlled territory overlaps with WHO response corridors in North Kivu. If M23 blocks burial teams or response personnel, Rwanda's existing perpetrator attribution extends to a secondary obstruction pathway. The July peak window is the critical monitoring checkpoint.

emergingRisks.risk: Anthropic's Congressional deadline for the US government to explain its export-control order falls June 26 — two days away.
emergingRisks.detail: Four bipartisan members of Congress demanded Commerce explain the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order by June 26. Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary. The government's response or non-response will be assessed next cycle. If the BIS order is modified or withdrawn, Anthropic's cost-of-conscience baseline could move.

emergingRisks.risk: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2 — about 39 days away. AI companies without published compliance plans face documented exposure under the new rules.
emergingRisks.detail: GPAI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling requirements, and whistleblower protections all activate on August 2. Anthropic and DeepMind have documented compliance infrastructure. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps. Post-applicability non-compliance constitutes a Tier 5 finding under the methodology.

emergingRisks.risk: The Sahel famine affects about 52.8 million people — the worst food crisis in a decade — across Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, and Nigeria.
emergingRisks.detail: The lean season peaks in August. Mali's calibration review (12.5 versus Burkina Faso's 6.3) is the scoring question tied to this risk. The Mali enforced-disappearance pattern and the food crisis are compounding. Niger and Chad have not been assessed this cycle; the Sahel-band reweight covers the full region.

emergingRisks.risk: Mali's government-perpetrated enforced-disappearance pattern — documented by UN Special Procedures as possibly constituting crimes against humanity — creates a calibration gap with already-reweighted Burkina Faso (6.3).
emergingRisks.detail: Burkina Faso was reweighted in June 2026 under the active-state-perpetration framework to 6.3. Mali carries a seed score of 12.5 despite comparable UN-documented conduct. The Sahel-band calibration review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. A coordinator decision could bring Mali's score into alignment with Burkina Faso. No timeline is set.

sectorAlerts.headline: Six countries confirmed at zero out of 100 — the largest floor cluster in the June 2026 series.
observations: Sudan, Israel, South Sudan, Yemen, and Russia confirm at the absolute floor of zero. This is the first cycle with five country-level floor entities all active simultaneously.
The Action (ACT) and Boundaries (BND) dimensions are at the lowest possible value across all six floor entities — meaning none corrects itself and none respects basic protections.
Yemen adds a new distinct category of floor evidence this cycle: deliberate targeting of the international humanitarian response infrastructure, not just civilians.
The attribution rule holds symmetrically: harm caused by Russia counts against Russia (not Ukraine); harm caused by Israel counts against Israel (not Lebanon). Both tested in this single cycle.

sectorAlerts.headline: UN investigators document enforced disappearances by Malian forces and Wagner — a Sahel calibration review is now open.
observations: Burkina Faso (6.3) was fully reassessed in June on comparable state-perpetration conduct. Mali (12.5) has not yet been reassessed.

The UN Special Procedures finding for Mali meets the evidence quality of the finding that triggered Burkina Faso's reweight — but the 'may constitute crimes against humanity' wording is pre-adjudication.
52.8 million people are food-insecure across the Sahel — the worst in a decade. The food crisis and the state-perpetration pattern are compounding.
Niger and Chad have not been assessed this cycle. A Sahel-band reweight would need to cover all three countries consistently.

sectorAlerts.headline: Anthropic's June 26 Congressional deadline is the most precisely dated forward event in the AI labs index this cycle.
observations: Anthropic (59.1 of 100, Functional) has been confirmed at this score for four consecutive cycles. It sits 0.9 points below the Established band boundary.

The US government's export-control order forced two Anthropic models offline. That action is attributed to the government. Anthropic's response — transparent disclosure, public dispute of proportionality — is consistent with its existing score.
xAI/Grok (0 of 100) carries three concurrent legal streams from prior cycles — none new this cycle, all still active.
EU AI Act full applicability is August 2 — 39 days away. The contrast between companies with documented compliance plans and those without is sharpening.

sectorAlerts.headline: Three false signals caught in one cycle — the highest per-cycle screening count in the June 2026 series.
observations: Rwanda: the scanner reported a stale score of 41.8. The actual published score is 27.3. A false upgrade anchored to a non-existent baseline was stopped.
Nigeria: the IPC Phase 5 Catastrophe evidence would have triggered a double-count of the same event scored on June 19. Stopped under the anti-double-count rule.
Mali: a one-off downgrade was stopped because the real issue is a cross-peer calibration gap, not an isolated event. Routed to coordinator-level review instead.
The Rwanda stale-baseline has now triggered a false positive in two consecutive cycles (June 23 and June 24). The scanner's baseline cache for Rwanda should be updated.

methodologyNotes.name: Who We Score vs. Who Suffered the Harm
methodologyNotes.description: The benchmark scores the entity that caused harm — not the one that suffered it. Russia's strikes on Ukraine count against Russia, not Ukraine. Israel's military operations in Lebanon count against Israel, not Lebanon. Houthi detentions of UN staff count against the Houthi de-facto authority, not against Yemen as a victim. This principle was applied consistently to multiple entities in this cycle.

methodologyNotes.name: How We Stop False Score Changes: the Screening Check
methodologyNotes.description: Before any score change is proposed, the evidence goes through a screening check. Three tests apply: (1) Is the starting score based on an accurate, current figure — or a stale one? This stopped a false Rwanda upgrade this cycle. (2) Has this evidence already been counted in a recent score change? This stopped a false Nigeria downgrade — the Borno evidence was already used on June 19. (3) Is the finding a cross-peer calibration question rather than a single-entity event? This stopped a false Mali downgrade and routed it correctly to a broader Sahel review.

methodologyNotes.name: Not Yet Decided in Court: How Pre-Adjudication Evidence Works
methodologyNotes.description: When a court case, investigation, or legal finding has not yet concluded, the benchmark does not change a score based on it. The UN Special Procedures finding for Mali says enforced disappearances 'may constitute crimes against humanity' — serious, but not yet adjudicated. The benchmark notes this evidence and opens a calibration review, but does not apply a score change until the finding is concluded.

methodologyNotes.name: Starting-Point Scores vs. Evidence-Based Scores
methodologyNotes.description: Five rotation-backfill countries (Kosovo, Malawi, Côte d'Ivoire, Liberia, Benin) confirmed at 35.9 out of 100 — but this is a starting-point seed score, not a score based on a full evidence review. No recent conduct evidence was found for these countries. The seed score says 'we have not yet assessed this country from evidence.' It is not a quality judgment. Full evidence-based assessments for these countries remain outstanding.

forwardTriggers.trigger: Congressional deadline for US Commerce to explain the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 export-control order. The government's response will be assessed next cycle.
July natural Bundibugyo Ebola peak window. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors is the secondary trigger for attributing obstruction to Rwanda.
Full resolution-phase reassessment due. Conversion trigger: reversal of June 8 codified-impunity law and release of detained union leaders.
EU AI Act full applicability — GPAI transparency, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. No public compliance roadmap from xAI.
August IPC lean-season update — Phase 5 Borno trajectory and government response adequacy.
Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review; Mali (12.5) versus reweighted peer Burkina Faso (6.3) on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: If Mali's Malian Armed Forces and Wagner enforced-disappearance pattern meets the same UN Special Procedures evidence threshold that triggered Burkina Faso's reweight to 6.3, does the Accountability dimension hold at 1.5 — or does it flow to the active-state-perpetration anchor?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Same UN evidence standard, two Sahel neighbors, different scores.
